file_name: Rupa et al. - 2024 - Melanoma detection using convolutional neural networks.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), a deep learning technique, to detect melanoma (a dermatology/skin cancer task). Specifically, it employs a custom CNN architecture with 2 CNN layers, 1 Maxpool layer, and a dropout layer on the ISIC Archive 2017 dataset to classify images as benign or malignant melanoma, achieving 88.89% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work using a custom CNN architecture on the ISIC Archive 2017 dataset to detect melanoma. It includes a 'Proposed Methodology' section detailing the model structure (2 CNN layers, 1 Maxpool layer, dropout), an 'Experimental Results' section reporting specific accuracy and loss metrics for various epoch and batch size configurations, and a 'Conclusion' summarizing the new findings (88.89% accuracy). Although it contains a literature review section, the primary contribution is the presentation of new empirical results from a diagnostic accuracy study.

title: Melanoma Detection using Convolutional Neural Networks
journal: International Journal of Innovative Science and Research Technology
brief_summary: This study addresses the detection of melanoma, a dangerous form of skin cancer, using a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture. The researchers utilized the ISIC Archive 2017 dataset, which contains 2000 training and 600 testing images, to classify lesions as benign or malignant. The proposed model consists of two CNN layers with 32 and 64 filters, a max-pooling layer, a dropout layer, and a fully connected layer with sigmoid activation for binary classification. The evaluation involved testing various combinations of epochs and batch sizes to optimize performance. The highest accuracy of 88.89% was achieved with 45 epochs and a batch size of 2, demonstrating the effectiveness of the custom CNN for melanoma detection.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on detecting melanoma using the ISIC Archive 2017 dataset. The classification task is binary, distinguishing between benign and malignant melanoma. The paper notes that the ISIC dataset contains images of 7 types of skin cancers, but the experiment specifically targets melanoma classification.
Mentioned in the abstract as one of the three basic types of skin cancer, but not studied or used in the experiments.
Mentioned in the abstract as one of the three basic types of skin cancer, but not studied or used in the experiments.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of melanoma lesions into Benign Melanoma and Malignant Melanoma categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'binary classification' using a sigmoid activation function. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (Benign vs. Malignant) using a custom CNN architecture on the ISIC Archive 2017 dataset. Performance is measured by accuracy (e.g., 88.89%).

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive 2017
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions, including ground truth images and metadata. The dataset contains images of 7 types of skin cancers, but this study specifically utilized the melanoma subset. Images were resized to 100x100 pixels for the proposed CNN model.

use.training: Used for training the custom CNN architecture with various epoch and batch size configurations.
use.testing: Used for testing the model performance to calculate accuracy and loss metrics.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. It reports descriptive performance metrics (accuracy and loss) for different hyperparameter configurations.

ISIC Archive 2017.notes: The paper reports a discrepancy in the Conclusion section stating 'batch size 3' for the 88.89% accuracy result, but the Experimental Results section (Fig 3 and Table 1) consistently identifies the best performance as occurring at Epochs=45 and Batch Size=2. The highest accuracy achieved was 88.89%.

layers.details: Connected to obtain highest training accuracy

architecture_details.intermediate_dimensions: First CNN layer output channel size: 396x644

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Referenced in the literature review (Nasr-Esfahani et al.) as a technique where images are rotated by 0, 90, 180, and 270 degrees.
Referenced in the literature review (Nasr-Esfahani et al.) as a technique used to synthesize new images from original ones.
The proposed methodology in this study did not employ data augmentation techniques; it utilized the ISIC Archive 2017 dataset directly with images resized to 100x100 pixels.

conclusions: The study concludes that using a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture is effective for detecting melanoma, achieving a highest accuracy of 88.89%.

The authors state that the highest accuracy of 88.89% was achieved using the ISIC Archive 2017 dataset with 45 epochs and a batch size of 3 (note: this contradicts the experimental results section which reports batch size 2 for this accuracy).
The study demonstrates that model performance varies with different combinations of epochs and batch sizes, with 88.89% accuracy being the optimal result among the tested configurations.
The paper does not explicitly acknowledge specific limitations such as dataset size, generalization to other populations, or clinical validation challenges in the conclusion section.
important_remarks: The paper contains a significant internal contradiction regarding the optimal hyperparameters: the Abstract, Experimental Results (Fig 3, Table 1), and Methodology sections state that 88.89% accuracy was achieved with a batch size of 2, whereas the Conclusion section incorrectly states the batch size was 3.

The study lacks external validation; it relies solely on the ISIC Archive 2017 dataset without splitting into training/validation/test sets or using cross-validation, raising concerns about overfitting and generalizability.

No data augmentation techniques were applied to the proposed custom CNN model, despite the literature review mentioning augmentation as a common practice, which may limit the model's robustness given the small dataset size (2000 training images).

The dataset used (ISIC Archive 2017) contains images of 7 types of skin cancers, but the study performs binary classification (Benign vs. Malignant Melanoma) without clarifying how non-melanoma classes were handled or excluded, potentially introducing class imbalance or leakage issues.

The paper does not report standard diagnostic metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, or AUC-ROC, relying exclusively on accuracy, which is insufficient for evaluating clinical diagnostic performance.

There is no discussion of limitations regarding dataset bias, demographic diversity, or clinical deployment challenges, nor are there any statistical significance tests (p-values) to support the reported performance differences.

The custom CNN architecture is very simple (only 2 convolutional layers) compared to state-of-the-art models mentioned in the literature review, and the achieved accuracy (88.89%) is lower than several pre-trained models cited in the review (e.g., Xception at 85.30% in a different study context, though direct comparison is limited by dataset/method differences).

The input image resolution was reduced to 100x100 pixels for the proposed model, which is significantly lower than the 224x224 pixels used for pre-trained models in the literature review, potentially discarding fine-grained diagnostic features.
